content.problemStatement:
Given NNN, AAA and BBB, find the value of the following expression:

∑i=0N∑j=0Ngcd(Ai+Bi,Aj+Bj)\sum_{i=0}^{N} \sum_{j=0}^{N} \text{gcd}(A^i + B^i, A^j + B^j)i=0∑N​j=0∑N​gcd(Ai+Bi,Aj+Bj)Since the value can be large, find it modulo (109+7)(10^9 + 7)(109+7).


Input Format

The first line of the input contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line containing three space-separated integers — AAA, BBB, and NNN.


Output Format

For each test case, output a single line containing the value of the expression modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 20), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers A, B and N.
A and B are positive, coprime (gcd(A, B) = 1) and B < A ≤ 10^9. N denotes the size of the sequence and satisfies 1 ≤ N ≤ 200000.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output a single line containing the value of
∑_{i=0}^{N-1} ∑_{j=0}^{N-1} gcd(A^i + B^i , A^j + B^j) modulo 1 000 000 007.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the summation expands to gcd(1,1)+gcd(5,1)+gcd(1,5)+gcd(5,5)=8. For the second test case, the summation expands to gcd(1,1)+gcd(3,1)+gcd(1,3)+gcd(3,3)=6.

solution.approach:
Let S_i = A^i + B^i (mod MOD). The required sum can be rewritten as
    Σ_{i=0}^{N-1} Σ_{j=0}^{N-1} f(gcd(i, j))
where f(d) = S_d. A classic number‑theoretic transformation gives
    Σ_{i=1}^{N} Σ_{j=1}^{N} f(gcd(i, j)) = Σ_{d=1}^{N} f(d) · C(N / d)
with C(m) = #{(x, y) | 1 ≤ x, y ≤ m, gcd(x, y) = 1}. Using the Möbius function μ we have
    C(m) = Σ_{t=1}^{m} μ[t] · ⌊m / t⌋².
The algorithm therefore consists of three parts:
1. Pre‑compute μ[ ] up to MAX = 200000 with a linear sieve (O(MAX)).
2. For each test case, pre‑compute S[d] = (A^d + B^d) mod MOD for all d = 1 … N using fast exponentiation in O(N log MOD).
3. Iterate d from 1 to N, grouping equal values of q = N / d. For each distinct q we compute C(q) by iterating over t in O(√q) using the same grouping technique. The total complexity per test case is O(N log N) which easily fits the limits (N ≤ 2·10⁵). All operations are performed modulo MOD, and modular exponentiation is used for A^d and B^d.
The final answer is the sum of f(d)·C(N/d) for d = 1 … N, taken modulo MOD.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (dominant work is the divisor‑grouped summation).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the arrays of μ and S.
